summary: Jeff Atwood shares his favorite programming quote—Nathaniel Borenstein's satirical observation about software engineers preferring to write a parameterized DestroyCity procedure rather than a hardcoded DestroyBaghdad one. He uses this as a launching point to discuss how programming quotes can serve as entry points to understanding the influential figures in computer science. Atwood highlights Larry Wall's wisdom about avoiding premature features and finding the 'long-term lazy way.' The post encourages developers to explore quotes as a way to discover their intellectual influences, while noting that respecting a person's insights doesn't require dogmatic acceptance of their tools.
key_points: Programming quotes serve as windows into understanding the notable personalities who shaped computer science
The Borenstein quote perfectly captures programmers' instinct toward abstraction and generalization, even in absurd contexts
Larry Wall's advice emphasizes patience in design: don't implement features immediately, and seek the long-term lazy approach over short-term shortcuts
Respecting someone's wisdom doesn't require adopting their specific tools or languages
Browsing quotes helps developers identify who their influences are—or should be
money_quotes: No ethically-trained software engineer would ever consent to write a DestroyBaghdad procedure. Basic professional ethics would instead require him to write a DestroyCity procedure, to which Baghdad could be given as a parameter.
Never have programmers been more neatly summarized.
Don't throw in a feature when you first think of it. Think if there's a way to generalize it, think if it should be generalized.
There is a level of abstraction beyond which people don't want to go.
Try to come up with the long-term lazy way, not the short-term lazy way.
I don't have to dogmatically accept Perl to respect Larry Wall.
It's not about the language; it's about learning to understand programmers as human beings.
key_insight: Programming quotes offer a gateway to understanding the giants of computer science as human beings, and you can learn from influential thinkers without dogmatically adopting their specific tools.
